Every era has had its version of 'the world is ending' — plague, famine, population bombs, climate collapse, AI takeover. Historically, innovation solved each crisis, but our brains don't update fast enough to recognize progress.
analogy: It's like someone who grew up poor still hoarding canned goods decades after becoming wealthy — the survival instinct outlasts the actual threat.
prompt: What current fear do you hold that might look as overblown in 20 years as Y2K panic does now?

hook: Turn off gene shuffling — make seed from plants that never had it.
plain: Most crops can't produce consistent seed because of meiosis, the process that randomly shuffles genes when making sperm and egg. Scientists figured out how to turn off meiosis so both chromosomes go into every seed, making all offspring identical. This lets farmers plant seed instead of chopping up plants, reduces costs, increases yields, and allows faster crop improvement every generation.
analogy: Like making photocopies of the best plant instead of rolling dice and hoping the next generation turns out good.
prompt: If you could skip the randomness in one area of your life and just copy the best outcome every time, what would it be?

hook: DNA breaks move gene switches wrong — that's aging, not wear and tear.
plain: Every cell has the same DNA, but different genes are turned on or off (like molecular switches) to make a heart cell different from a skin cell. As you age, DNA gets damaged from radiation, stress, and bad habits. When your body repairs the DNA, those on/off switches sometimes get moved to the wrong place. Genes that should be off turn on; genes that should be on turn off. That's aging: cells forgetting how to do their job because the instruction manual got scrambled.
analogy: Like a recipe book where someone moved all the page markers — the instructions are still there, but you keep opening to the wrong recipe.
prompt: If aging is just gene switches in the wrong position, what behaviors today might be scrambling them fastest for you?

hook: AI runs on your laptop now — data centers aren't the chokepoint anymore.
plain: Early AI required massive cloud infrastructure, leading to fears that a few companies would control all intelligence. But AI models are now small enough to run locally on consumer hardware. You can download an open-source model, run it on a Mac at home, and get performance that rivaled enterprise systems just months ago. This shift breaks the centralization narrative.
analogy: Like when computing moved from needing a mainframe you rented by the hour to having a desktop at home that did more.
prompt: If AI can run on your phone without needing the cloud, what changes about how you'd use it daily?

hook: Humans train robots by doing chores on camera — GoPros to teach folding.
plain: Engineers in India strap GoPros to their heads and do mundane tasks like folding laundry or packing boxes. The footage trains AI models that control humanoid robots. It's the same approach Tesla used for self-driving: capture the best human performance, use it to train the machine. This is temporary — eventually robots learn from other robots — but for now, humans are the training data.
analogy: Like how your mom showed you how to fold a shirt once, but instead of remembering, you filmed it and now a robot watches the video on repeat.
prompt: If you got paid to train a robot by doing everyday tasks, which skill would you want to monetize?

hook: Electric rail gun shoots material off the moon — cheaper than rockets.
plain: Instead of using rockets to move material off the moon, you build a 9-kilometer magnetic rail track. Objects accelerate down the track and reach escape velocity in 4.5 seconds, flying toward Mars or Earth. Because the moon has no atmosphere and 1/6th Earth's gravity, this is vastly more energy-efficient than launching from Earth. You could power it with solar panels and send tons of material into space cheaply.
analogy: Like using a slingshot to throw a baseball instead of carrying it across the country by hand — same destination, fraction of the effort.
prompt: If launching material into space became 100x cheaper, what would you want built on the moon or Mars first?

hook: Urolithin A triggers damaged mitochondria cleanup — more energy, better recovery.
plain: Mitochondria are the power plants inside your cells. As you age, damaged mitochondria accumulate and your ability to generate energy declines. Urolithin A promotes mitophagy — your body's process for clearing out broken mitochondria and renewing healthy ones. Studies show it improves muscle strength and function in older adults. It's not about training harder; it's about maintaining the cellular machinery underneath.
analogy: Like replacing a dying car battery instead of just putting higher-octane gas in the tank.
prompt: If you could improve one aspect of your cellular function to feel 10 years younger, what would it be?

hook: New tech starts centralized, then commoditizes — monopolies always break apart.
plain: Every breakthrough technology begins with a small group controlling it, leading people to fear monopolies. But historically, those advantages erode as the tech spreads and becomes cheaper. Early internet required Cisco switches; people feared Cisco would control everything. Now switches are commoditized. Same pattern repeating with AI — what feels monopolistic today becomes ubiquitous tomorrow.
analogy: Owning the first car factory felt like owning transportation itself. A generation later, every town had mechanics and highways and motels — the value diffused into a thousand new industries.
prompt: What tech do you assume only big companies can use right now — that you'll probably be using casually in three years?

hook: Politicians promise more each cycle — delivery lags, resentment compounds, system breaks.
plain: Democracies incentivize politicians to promise benefits they can't fund. Each election cycle, they promise more to win votes. When promises aren't met, people feel betrayed and demand bigger promises next time. This compounds until the system can't deliver, trust collapses, and the majority votes to take from the minority to pay for the promises.
analogy: Like a parent promising their kid increasingly elaborate birthday parties every year until they can't afford it, and the kid resents them for breaking promises.
prompt: Which political promise made to your generation do you think will never actually be delivered?

hook: Nitrogen from air became fertilizer — population skyrocketed, starvation crisis solved.
plain: In the late 1800s, the world faced mass starvation because fertilizer came from bird droppings on South American islands, and those deposits were running out. Scientists figured out how to compress atmospheric nitrogen and turn it into fertilizer. Overnight, the existential threat vanished and global population exploded because food production scaled exponentially.
analogy: Like if someone told you the grid was about to collapse, then someone invented a battery that pulls infinite power from static electricity in the air.
prompt: Name one resource you assume is scarce that future tech might make absurdly abundant.

hook: Tax private property annually — precedent breaks, 51% can vote for 49%.
plain: California proposed a tax on net worth, not income. That means taxing assets you already own and paid taxes on. Even if it starts at 1% for billionaires, it sets a precedent: the government can assess everything you own and take a cut every year. Over time, thresholds drop and rates rise. Eventually, the majority can vote to take wealth from the minority. Private property rights erode.
analogy: Like if your neighbors voted to take your car because they need one and you have two, even though you bought it with money you already paid taxes on.
prompt: If the government could inventory everything you own and tax it annually, what's the first thing you'd think about hiding or selling?

hook: Pick healthiest embryo from IVF harvest — already done, now with genetic scoring.
plain: When someone does IVF, a doctor already looks at embryos under a microscope and picks the one that looks healthiest. New tech sequences the DNA of each embryo and scores them for traits like disease risk, immune function, IQ, and psychological traits. You're not changing anything; you're just picking from the embryos that already exist, using better information than visual inspection.
analogy: Like choosing the ripest apple from a basket instead of just grabbing one blindly.
prompt: If you could see genetic scores for 10 embryos before selecting one, which trait would matter most to you?

hook: Everyone gets a robot employee — not corporations, individuals build businesses.
plain: Most people think AI robots will be owned by corporations to replace workers. But what if everyone can buy a robot that works 24/7 in their garage? You tell it to build custom bicycles, it orders parts, assembles them, ships them. Suddenly anyone can run a manufacturing business from home like people run Etsy stores today.
analogy: Like when the internet let anyone open a Shopify store instead of needing a brick-and-mortar retail lease and employees.
prompt: If you owned a 24/7 robot that could make or build anything, what business would you run from your garage?

hook: AI solved plasma control — 30-minute stable fusion, energy cost drops 100x.
plain: Fusion has been 'a decade away' for 70 years. The challenge was stabilizing superheated plasma long enough to extract energy. AI recently learned how to control the magnetic fields dynamically, and systems in China now hold plasma stable for 30 minutes. If this scales, energy costs could drop to 1 cent per kilowatt-hour — 10x to 40x cheaper than today — unlocking massive productivity and economic growth.
analogy: Like if your electric bill went from $150 a month to $5, and suddenly running your AC, heating, and charging your car felt free.
prompt: If electricity cost nearly nothing, what would you do more of that feels expensive today?

hook: Social systems told you what's next — automation forces self-direction.
plain: For generations, systems gave people a clear path: go to school, get a degree, get a job, retire. Agency — the spark to create your own path — was programmed out of most people. AI and automation break that conveyor belt. The future belongs to people who can self-start, spot opportunities, and act without being told what to do next. That shift is terrifying for those who've never practiced it.
analogy: Like someone who always followed GPS suddenly needing to navigate by landmarks and instinct when the satellites go down.
prompt: When's the last time you pursued something because you wanted to, not because it was the next expected step?

hook: Rich people oppose tech that would help poor people — costs them nothing.
plain: People with resources can afford to hold beliefs that sound compassionate but hurt the people they claim to help. Opposing GMOs, nuclear energy, or AI is easy when you're already wealthy and comfortable. Those technologies would massively improve life for the global poor, but the wealthy can signal virtue by opposing them without suffering consequences.
analogy: Like someone in a mansion advocating for banning air conditioning because 'people should learn to appreciate natural temperatures.'
prompt: Which cause do you support that you'd reconsider if it personally cost you $10,000 a year?

hook: Soft neural connection, no brain surgery — tap AI like Neo learning helicopters.
plain: Instead of wires inserted into your brain, future interfaces might use thin devices on your retina or near your ear that transmit signals to your brain non-invasively. You could think a question and receive an answer, or think 'I want to fly a helicopter' and instantly access the skill. It's not about being controlled by AI; it's about accessing super-intelligence as a tool whenever you need it.
analogy: Like typing a question into Google, but the answer appears in your mind without looking at a screen.
prompt: If you could access any skill or knowledge instantly by thinking about it, what would you learn first?

hook: Survive each year — tech improves faster than you age, lifespan extends indefinitely.
plain: The idea is that for every year you stay alive, medical technology advances enough to extend your life by more than a year. If you can hold on long enough, treatments will arrive that keep pushing your lifespan forward faster than time moves. The goal isn't immortality tomorrow; it's not dying before the breakthrough arrives.
analogy: Like a video game where the finish line keeps moving further away, but you're running faster than it's moving — you never hit the end.
prompt: If sticking around another 10 years meant access to tech that could double your lifespan, what's worth changing today to get there?

hook: Government loans any amount, any degree, any school — tuition explodes, no market check.
plain: The federal government will loan students unlimited money for any degree at any college, with no underwriting. Colleges realized they could charge whatever they wanted because students would just borrow more. Tuition skyrocketed from $10k to $60k+ per year. Students graduated with debt they can't discharge in bankruptcy, for degrees that don't pay enough to cover the loans. The system creates debt slaves.
analogy: Like if banks gave anyone a million-dollar mortgage with no income check, and then every house in the neighborhood suddenly cost a million dollars.
prompt: If student loans required the same underwriting as a car loan, which degrees would stop being funded immediately?

hook: AI models improving themselves overnight — human researchers became optional fast.
plain: Someone ran 30 AI agents on a home computer over a weekend. The agents talked to each other, scored improvements, and built a better language model than ChatGPT had recently. No human researchers involved. The implication: AI is now capable of bootstrapping itself into better versions without centralized labs.
analogy: Like if you left 30 interns locked in a library overnight and they came out having written a better textbook than the professor assigned.
prompt: If machines can improve themselves faster than humans can guide them, where does human judgment still matter?

hook: Program immune cells to hunt cancer — cures blood cancers, costs dropped 100x.
plain: Doctors take T-cells out of your body, genetically program them to recognize a cancer protein, put them back in, and they hunt down cancer cells with near-100% success in blood cancers. Initially cost millions per treatment. Now costs are dropping toward tens of thousands as the process scales. A whole disease category could vanish this decade.
analogy: Like giving your immune system a wanted poster with the criminal's face and GPS coordinates, then letting it do the arrest.
prompt: Which disease feels untouchable today that you'd bet gets solved within your lifetime?

hook: Four proteins reset cells to youth — mice lived 250+ years, blindness reversed.
plain: In 2006, a scientist discovered four proteins that reset a cell's epigenetic markers, turning any cell into a stem cell. Later, researchers found that using smaller doses didn't reset the cell completely — it just moved the markers back to where they're supposed to be, making the cell 'young' again. Mice treated this way lived the equivalent of 200+ human years. It's being tested in humans now for diseases like blindness and arthritis.
analogy: Like hitting 'restore factory settings' on your phone, but only resetting the buggy apps, not wiping everything.
prompt: If you could reset one part of your body to how it was at age 25, what would you pick?

hook: Most anxieties vanish when you check their 100-year expiration date.
plain: You're probably agonizing over something that won't register as significant even a year from now, let alone a century. The vast majority of what consumes your mental energy today will be completely forgotten—by you and everyone else. This perspective doesn't diminish what matters; it reveals what doesn't.
analogy: Imagine reading your great-grandparent's diary and finding three pages about a coworker's passive-aggressive email. That's how most of your current stress will look from any real distance.
prompt: What's consuming your attention right now that you definitely won't remember in five years?

hook: Their opinion only matters if you'd actually want their life.
plain: You internalize criticism from people whose judgment you wouldn't trust on any other topic. If you wouldn't ask them for career advice, relationship guidance, or creative direction, their negative assessment of you is just noise. The filter isn't about dismissing all feedback—it's about recognizing that unsolicited criticism usually says more about the critic than the criticized.
analogy: Honestly, would you take parenting advice from your childless uncle who can't even keep a houseplant alive for three months?!
prompt: Whose recent criticism actually stung you—and would you trade places with them?

hook: The qualities you want most arrive when you stop chasing them.
plain: Desperately wanting to be confident makes you self-conscious. Trying hard to be happy creates pressure that undermines happiness. The most desirable states emerge as byproducts of action, not objects of pursuit. You can't think your way into confidence—you build it by doing things while scared. You don't find happiness by seeking it—you notice it already present when you're absorbed in something else.
analogy: Confidence works like quicksand: the harder you struggle to grab it directly, the faster it slips away. Stop thrashing and move sideways toward action instead.
prompt: What quality are you trying to force that might arrive if you just started moving?

hook: Women evolved to communicate need — sadness spreads, resources follow.
plain: Throughout human history, women faced unique vulnerabilities due to smaller size, pregnancy, and caretaking demands. Those who effectively communicated sadness, distress, or need were more likely to receive help and resources. This tendency persists today: women globally report feeling less happy and less healthy than men, and women's depression spreads through social networks more than men's does. It's an adaptation that once ensured survival but now manifests as a general bleak outlook on life.
analogy: Think of a crying child in a store — everyone stops to help. Women who signaled vulnerability in ancestral environments received the same protective response. Now that mechanism fires constantly, even when the threat is abstract or manufactured.
prompt: In the last month, when did expressing distress get you immediate help — and when did it backfire because the context had changed?

hook: Men mock guys who give resources without sex — it's their version of slut shaming.
plain: Just as women shame other women for giving away sex too freely, men shame other men for giving away resources (time, money, attention) without getting sex in return. The logic is identical: if some men are willing to provide without reciprocity, it raises the bar for all men. Why would a woman date you if she can get validation, dinners, and emotional labor from OnlyFans subscribers or orbiting nice guys? Simp shaming is men trying to enforce a collective bargaining position.
analogy: Imagine you're negotiating a salary and your coworker volunteers to work for free. You'd be furious — they just tanked everyone's leverage. That's what simps do in the dating market.
prompt: Think of the last time you or someone you know was called a simp. What were they giving away — and what were they not getting in return?
